1.description: Fraction of cash-out and the median rate ratio for refinance loans. Panel A plots the percentage of refinancing resulting in a 5% higher loan amount (cash-out), no change, or lower loan amount (pay-down). Panel B plots the median ratio of new to old loan rates upon refinancing, with the normalized house price index (Case-Shiller index divided by nominal GDP, gray dotted line) in the background. Refinancing data are from Freddie Mac for the period 1985Q1 to 2012Q4. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

2.description: Time series of exogenous state variables. Solid lines: data; circles: corresponding approximated values in the model. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

3.description: Cross-sectional implications: model versus data. Model estimates are based on a pooled simulated panel; data analogs are based on pooled SCF data from 1998 to 2010. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

4.description: Refinancing for the cross-section of households. Model estimates are based on a pooled simulated panel; data analogs are based on pooled PSID data for 1999 to 2013. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

5.description: Refinancing and liquid assets: before and after. Model estimates are based on a pooled simulated panel; data analogs are based on pooled PSID data from 1999 to 2013. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

6.description: Model-implied aggregate time series. This figure plots the model-implied aggregate time series (solid lines) of real consumption growth and the median rate ratio of refinance loans and their data counterparts (dashed lines). [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

7.description: Household balance sheets and refinancing, 2001 to 2013: model versus data. The dash-diamond, solid-cross, and dot-square lines represent, respectively, the top, middle, and bottom quintiles of the distribution of debt-to-income ratio in 2001. The left panels are based on model simulation, while the right panels are based on PSID data. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

8.description: Cumulative consumption growth relative to 2007. The three panels show the cumulative consumption growth for the cross-section of households sorted on LTI, LTV, and house value to total wealth in 2007. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]

9.description: Replicating Mian and Sufi (2011) evidence on household debt. This figure plots simulated dynamics of house price growth (Panel A), total household debt growth (Panel B), and the change in (average) debt-to-income ratios (Panel C) from 2001 to 2008, as implied by our model, which correspond to the same panels in figure 2 in Mian and Sufi (2011). Solid lines correspond the baseline case of the estimated model, the others represent counterfactual scenarios that use the same model parameters but varying specification of house processes and financing constraints. The dashed lines (“inelastic supply”) indicate the case where house prices are twice as volatile (relative to average income) as the baseline; the dotted lines (“elastic supply”) indicate the case with house prices that are proportional to aggregate income; squares indicate the case where house prices are as in the baseline scenario but collateral constraints are relaxed, with the maximum loan-to-value ratio (LTV) at 100% and no labor-to-income (LTI) constraint. Right-facing and left-facing triangles correspond to the cases with the relaxed collateral constraints as above where house prices are as in the “elastic” and “inelastic” cases, respectively. The solid line represents the case with the house price path from the baseline simulation. [Color figure can be viewed atwileyonlinelibrary.com]
